literal: fashioning, measuring-out, world-forming power; often connected with √mā 'to measure, to form'
invariant_definition: *Māyā* has no single pan-Indian meaning. The thinnest common core is this: it names a power or condition through which manifest plurality is produced, sustained, or experienced. Beyond that, the term becomes school-specific. In Vedic and purāṇic usage it can mean divine skill, wondrous power, or formative capacity. In Advaita it becomes the cosmic register of *avidyā*, the principle by which the non-dual appears as manifold without undergoing real modification. In realist Vedāntas it names a real divine power, often identified with *prakṛti* or with the Lord's external energy. In Trika it is not illusion at all but a real self-contractive phase of Śiva's *svātantrya*, linked to the *kañcuka* analysis and the production of differentiated subjects and objects. The term therefore sits amid disputes over causality, *pariṇāma* versus *vivarta*, the status of the world, and the relation between God and manifestation. In practice *māyā* explains embodiment, limitation, and cosmic display, but what sort of explanation this is depends entirely on the metaphysics of the school using it.

school_framing.shared_core: *Māyā* names **the power by which the One appears as many; the veiling-projecting structure that lets phenomenal multiplicity stand without compromising the unity of its source** (Śvetāśvatara 4.10; *māyāṃ tu prakṛtiṃ vidyāt māyinaṃ tu maheśvaram*). Schools that retain the term agree on this structural role; they disagree on whether *māyā* is *brahman*'s own real *śakti* (Vaiṣṇava schools, Trika), Brahman's *anirvacanīya* upādhi (Advaita), or simply *prakṛti* (Sāṅkhya-Bhikṣu reading).
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Real disagreement** between Advaita (*māyā* as *anirvacanīya*, *mithyā*, sublatable by *jñāna*) and Tattva-vāda (*māyā* as *Viṣṇu-saṅkalpa*, real, never sublated; *Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya* 3.21–22) — same M-register, same scope-inside, opposing claims (AF1). **Same concept, different aspect** when the schools describe māyā's epistemic effect on *jīva* versus its causal role in cosmogony.

per_school.definition: *Māyā* is the beginningless sleep of the *jīva* and the sole register in which duality appears, so that *prapañca* is *māyāmātra* while *advaita* alone is *paramārtha* [[MK 1.16-18](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakaraṇa.16-18)]. The unborn is differentiated only *māyayā*, never *tattvataḥ*; real difference would compromise its *ajatva* and *amṛtatva* [[MK 3.19-21](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.19-21)]. Post-Śaṅkara Advaita identifies this *māyā* with *avidyā*: neither *sat* nor *asat*, but *anirvacanīyā*, operative in *vyavahāra* and located in the *jīvas* [[BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-avidy)]. In Vivaraṇa idiom, causal Brahman is Brahman qualified by *anirvacanīya-māyā*, and the same doctrine can be restated as *māyā* being *sadasadbhyām anirvacanīyā* [[PV p. 212](cite://prakasatman/pancapadika-vivarana/brahmaṇo lakṣaṇopalakṣaṇe), [IṢ 1.1](cite://vimuktatman/ista-siddhi/1.Prapañcasyānirvacanīyatvam)].

citations.locus_short: BS 1.1 (avidyā neither sat nor asat; in the jīvas)

per_school.definition: The on-disk Viśiṣṭādvaitin dossier handles *māyā* chiefly by rejecting the thesis that Brahman itself, ignorant and overrun by alien *upādhis*, becomes the saṃsārin; that teaching is condemned as *śruti-nyāyāpeta* [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §2](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§2)]. Positively, Bhagavān Nārāyaṇa is the *antaryāmin* of the entire *cid-acid-prapañca*, taught through expressions such as "his power," "his body," and "his manifestation"; the world-order is therefore a real dependence-structure, not a merely apparent projection [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)]. Beginningless ignorance belongs to the embodied *jīva*'s karmic entanglement and is remedied through knowledge of the *dehātirikta ātman* and upāsanā of the indwelling Lord, not by canceling the reality of the cosmos [[Vedārtha-Saṃgraha §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4)].

When the term is accepted positively, *māyā* is Hari's own power: *ātma-māyā* is glossed as *svarūpabhūtecchā*, a will intrinsic to the Lord rather than an ontic screen over reality [[Bhāg. 1.1.18](cite://madhva/bhagavata-tatparya-nirnaya/1.1.18), [Bhāg. 1.1.18 — gloss](cite://madhva/bhagavata-tatparya-nirnaya/1.1.18 — gloss)]. Polemically, no *ajñāna* can coherently function as an *āvaraṇa*, since there is no admissible unestablished substrate for it to cover [[Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, "Nothing can serve as a covering ignorance"](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/can serve as a covering ignorance)]. For that reason the entire *māyāvāda* is rejected as incoherent, and the world's difference is not downgraded to a merely apparent status [[Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, "Therefore the whole doctrine is set aside"](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/the whole doctrine is set aside), [Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, concluding verse](cite://madhva/mayavada-khandana/verse of refutation)].
primary_loci: Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, 'Nothing can serve as a covering ignorance'
Māyāvāda-Khaṇḍana, 'Therefore the whole doctrine is set aside'

per_school.definition: The *abheda-śāstras* are retained only after the rival *pariccheda* and *pratibimba* readings are subordinated to Bhagavān's *svābhāvika acintya-śakti* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42), [Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Jīvas are *cid-rūpa* yet stand to Bhagavān as ray-atoms to the sun, so *bheda* and *avyatireka* are both preserved without collapsing into *svarūpābheda* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Within that settlement, Bhagavān is *māyāśraya* and the bound *jīva* is *māyā-mohita*; precisely this difference makes *bhagavad-bhajana* the school's *abhidheya* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)].

Nimbārka describes the *jīva* as bearing a form associated with beginningless *māyā*; bondage, devotion, and release are real conditions of that dependent self and are known only by Bhagavān's grace [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2)]. On the side of *acit*, the insentient order is explicitly denoted by terms such as *māyā* and *pradhāna* [[DaŚ 3](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/3)]. *Māyā* therefore names a real, dependent non-conscious principle within the *bhedābheda* order, not an *anirvacanīya* cancellation of the world's reality [[DaŚ 2](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/2), [DaŚ 3](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/3)].

Śuddhādvaita refuses to make *nāma-rūpa* into an illusory screen: Brahman itself is the *samavāyi-kāraṇa*, while name and form are *kārya-dharmas* [[BSB 1.1.4](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.4)]. The world is a real *pariṇāma* of Brahman, though Brahman remains *avikṛta* in the alaukika manner illustrated by gold and its products [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)]. *Avidyā-nivṛtti* prepares the *jīva* for Puruṣottama, but attainment is completed through *bhakti* and *varaṇa* rather than by sublating a *mithyā-jagat* [[BSB 1.1.12-19](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.1.12-19)].

Classical Sāṅkhya does not make *māyā* a load-bearing cosmological principle; the work later attached to that term is assigned instead to *pradhāna/prakṛti*, whose activity proceeds for the sake of the *puruṣa*'s release [[SK 57](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/57)]. Bondage and release are not states of *puruṣa* but processes of *prakṛti* itself, which alone 'transmigrates,' 'is bound,' and 'is released' through its own forms [[SK 62](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/62)][[SK 63](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/63)]. The nearest functional analogue to *māyā* is therefore *viparyaya* or non-discriminative entanglement, removed by the knowledge expressed as 'I am not, nothing is mine, I am not this,' after which *prakṛti* ceases its productive unfolding [[SK 64](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/64)][[SK 65](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/65)].

Pātañjala Yoga does not elevate *māyā* into an ontic world-principle; bondage is explained through *avidyā*, the misapprehension of the non-eternal, impure, painful, and non-self as their opposites [[YS 2.5](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.5)]. That *avidyā* is the cause of the *saṃyoga* of seer and seen, while uninterrupted *vivekakhyāti* is the means of its removal [[YS 2.24](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.24)][[YS 2.26](cite://patanjali/yoga-sutra/2.26)]. When the term itself appears in the bhāṣya, it functions as simile rather than ontology: what has entered the field of perception is said to be 'like māyā,' exceedingly insubstantial, by comparison with the guṇa-s' subtler condition [[YBh 4.13](cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/4.13)].

Vijñānabhikṣu explicitly glosses *māyā* as *prakṛti* and *māyin* as Maheśvara, so the term denotes the cosmological non-conscious principle rather than an epistemic indeterminable [[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)]. This is the late synthetic Sāṅkhya-Yoga-Vedānta line preserved on disk: *māyā* is relocated into the ontology of *prakṛti*-governed manifestation under the Lord [[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)].

For Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, *māyā* is not a world-grounding principle but an example within the analysis of false cognition: the pūrvapakṣa likens apparent objecthood to dream, magical illusion, gandharva-city, and mirage, and the siddhānta answers that false apprehension is destroyed by *tattvajñāna* [[NyS 4.2.32](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/4.2.32)][[NyS 4.2.35](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/4.2.35)]. Systematically, bondage proceeds from *mithyājñāna* and ceases by its successive removal, so error is an epistemic-causal defect rather than a quasi-substantial power [[NyS 1.1.2](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.2)]. Vaiśeṣika states the same point in its own idiom by defining *avidyā* as defective cognition arising from sense-defect and saṃskāra, opposed to *vidyā* as non-defective cognition [[VS 9.25-27](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/9.25-27)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: In the listed Jaimini-Śabara-Kumārila corpus, *māyā* does not function as a technical school-category; the doctrinal load falls instead on ritual-hermeneutic categories such as *dharma*, *codanā*, *śeṣa*, and allied interpretive devices.

per_school.definition: In Mādhyamaka, *māyā* functions as a paradigmatic simile for dependently arisen phenomena precisely because dependent arising excludes *svabhāva*; origination, duration, and cessation are 'like māyā, like a dream, like a gandharva-city' [[MMK 7.34](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/7.34)][[MMK 24.18](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/24.18)]. The comparison does not posit an underlying illusion-substance; rather, auspicious and inauspicious constructions fail with respect to entities that are like magical persons and reflections [[MMK 23.9](cite://nagarjuna/mula-madhyamaka-karika/23.9)]. Nāgārjuna states the governing principle without remainder in the *Vigraha-vyāvartanī*: whatever is grasped dependently is precisely emptiness [[VV 66](cite://nagarjuna/vigraha-vyavartani/66)].

In the on-disk Yogācāra dossier, *māyā* is not the load-bearing cosmological term; misleading objecthood is analyzed through *asadarthāvabhāsa* and *vijñaptimātratā* [[Vvs 1](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/1)]. Dream, preta-vision, and hell-experience show that *deśa-kāla-niyama*, *saṃtānāniyama*, and even efficacy can obtain although the appearing object is unreal [[Vvs 3](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Preta Replies), [Vvs 4](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/and Hell)]. What ordinary thought takes as object and subject is *parikalpita* alone, so the explanatory work later polemics often assign to *māyā* is here redistributed to *vikalpa*, *bīja*, and *vijñāna-pariṇāma* [[Triṃśikā 17](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/17), [Triṃśikā 20](cite://vasubandhu/trimsika/20), [Vvs 10](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/10 and Entry into *Dharma-Nairātmya*)].

In Pratyabhijñā/Trika, *māyā* is a real phase of Śiva's self-contraction rather than an illusory principle external to consciousness; the finite *aṇu* is described as inwardly furnished with māyā together with the six *kañcukas* [[PS 17](cite://abhinavagupta/paramartha-sara/17)]. Kṣemarāja accordingly defines the *māyāpramātā* through its citta-constitution and explains bondage as the contraction of the Lord's own powers into *māyīya mala* and the limiting forms of *kalā, vidyā, rāga, kāla,* and *niyati* [[PH 6](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/6)][[PH 9](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9)]. The function of *māyā* is thus differential manifestation through *bhedavyāpti*, while liberation is the re-expansion of that contracted subject in *pratyabhijñā* as Śiva himself [[PH 9](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9)].

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'illusion.' That is seriously misleading. Most schools using *māyā* do not mean subjective illusion at all, and even Advaita does not mean simple unreality or *asat*. The word can import very different metaphysics: cosmic ignorance, real divine power, material nature, or Śiva's self-contraction. English should therefore signal the school-specific use instead of flattening everything into 'illusion.'
